message.content: write a docuement to explain the new Kanata loop-window pipeline dump feature for usage.

attachment.content:
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, use Config tool.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- simplify: Review changed code for reuse, quality, and efficiency, then fix any issues found.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled remote agents (triggers) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring remote agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/triggers.
- claude-api: Build, debug, and optimize Claude API / Anthropic SDK apps. Apps built with this skill should include prompt caching. Also handles migrating existing Claude API code between Claude model versions (4.5 → 4.6, 4.6 → 4.7, retired-model replacements).
TRIGGER when: code imports `anthropic`/`@anthropic-ai/sdk`; user asks for the Claude API, Anthropic SDK, or Managed Agents; user adds/modifies/tunes a Claude feature (caching, thinking, compaction, tool use, batch, files, citations, memory) or model (Opus/Sonnet/Haiku) in a file; questions about prompt caching / cache hit rate in an Anthropic SDK project.
SKIP: file imports `openai`/other-provider SDK, filename like `*-openai.py`/`*-generic.py`, provider-neutral code, general programming/ML.
- skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- libnn-wiki-writer: Internal style guide for writing docs/wiki/ pages during /libnn-optimizer learn phase. Not a standalone skill -- read by the optimize agent.
- mlir-transform-writer: Write MLIR transform dialect scripts that directly control IR lowering - tiling, fusion, vectorization, bufferization, GPU thread mapping, shared memory promotion, and more. Use this skill whenever someone asks to generate, write, or modify a transform dialect script, transform library, codegen strategy, or tuning spec in MLIR. Also trigger when the request involves composing IR transformations for linalg ops, controlling IREE codegen pipelines via transform dialect, or replacing annotation-only configs with full transform strategies. Covers both upstream MLIR transform ops and IREE-specific extensions.
- iree-optimizer: Agentic per-dispatch tile-size tuner for IREE CPU codegen on RISC-V. Keeps IREE's chosen pipeline (CPUDoubleTilingExpert / CPUConvTileAndDecomposeExpert / CPUBufferOpsTileAndVectorize) and only overrides the root linalg op's lowering_config<tile_sizes = [...]>. Each round reads the best-so-far RISC-V assembly (LMUL, VLmax, register spills) to ground the next tile-size proposal; pre-compile divisibility gate ensures tiles divide their parent extents (no tail loop); a post-compile check of the IR after iree-codegen-generic-vectorization confirms no tail/masked main path. Compiles via iree_tuner.py, benchmarks on FPGA, iterates. Use whenever the user mentions tuning IREE dispatch tile sizes, agentic tile search, or wants to replace GA-based tile-size search with LLM-driven search. Trigger on "iree optimize", "optimize dispatch", "tile tune", or references to dispatch .mlir files for performance tuning.
- libnn-optimizer: Autonomous optimization workflow for libnn RISC-V neural network library functions
in the agentic_libnn_opt project. Use this skill when the user wants to optimize a
libnn function for FPGA performance, run the libnn optimization loop, validate the
optimization config, parse FPGA hardware features, extract shapes from perf test files,
or manage the optimization state machine. Trigger when: user mentions "optimize",
"libnn", "FPGA perf", "optimization loop", "validate config",
"phase gather/baseline/iterate/evaluate", "perf test", or references opt/config.toml,
opt/state.json, or any libnn function optimization task.
- cc-log: Lightweight system log to record all Claude Code behavior (tool use, subagent creation, compaction, permissions, session lifecycle) for analysis. Use when the user wants to enable/disable behavior logging, analyze Claude Code session logs, list saved logs, or understand Claude Code's tool usage patterns. Trigger on: "cc-log", "behavior log", "system log", "record behavior", "log enable", "log disable", "analyze log".
- ralph-loop:cancel-ralph: Cancel active Ralph Loop
- ralph-loop:ralph-loop: Start Ralph Loop in current session
- ralph-loop:help: Explain Ralph Loop plugin and available commands
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.content:
commit 8b53fc2f11991b87f28ee0bdf302ea94b7d84598
Author: Nick Hung <nick@andestech.com>
Date:   Thu Apr 30 06:27:47 2026 +0800

    feat: add Kanata loop-window pipeline dump
    
    Allow restricting Kanata/Clarity output to a steady-state window of a
    hot loop instead of the full simulation. New flags:
    --kanata-loop-start/-end (PCs of loop body), --kanata-loop-skip (warmup
    iterations to ignore), --kanata-loop-record (iterations to capture
    before exiting). Iteration count advances on retire of end_pc to
    ignore mispredicted/flushed fetches. Logger reports
    iters/insns/cycles/IPC for the recorded window.
    
    Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

 src/main.cpp                    |  63 ++++++++++++++++++++++-
 src/pipeview/clarity_viewer.hpp |  15 +++---
 src/pipeview/kanata_logger.hpp  | 108 +++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++++---
 src/pipeview/kanata_viewer.hpp  |   9 ++--
 src/simulator.hpp               |  10 ++--
 src/simulator_trace.hpp         |  10 ++--
 6 files changed, 187 insertions(+), 28 deletions(-)

1	## View the Output Log
2	To visualize the log generated from simulator, download `Konata` from [GitHub](https://github.com/shioyadan/Konata/tree/master). Follow the installation and usage instructions provided in the repository to analyze the log file.
3	
4	Here are some tips:
5	
6	Right click on your mouse and choose "hide flush"
7	![konata_hide_flush](fig/konata_hide_flush.png)
8	
9	To see IPC (instruction per cycle) and other useful statistics
10	![konata_stat](fig/konata_stat.png)
11	
12	## Scalar Pipeline Analysis Example
13	
14	For 45 series CPU, `konata.py` draws 5 scalar stages:
15	- __IS__: Issue
16	- __EX__: Execute
17	- __MM__: Memory
18	- __LX__: Late Execute
19	- __WB__: Write Back
20	
21	In below example, the loop jump is mispredicted in the first time (red underline), so several instructions are flushed (dark region) in between.
22	But in the next iteration, the jump instruction hits (yellow underline), so no flushed insn anymore.
23	
24	![konata_pipe](fig/konata_pipe.png)
25	
26	## Vector Pipeline Analysis Example
27	
28	For 45-series with VPU, you can see VPU stages
29	- __VQ__: enqueue to VIQ
30	- __VD__: dispatch to FU
31	- __VC__: FU commits
32	- __VW1__: Write to 1st VRF
33	- __VW2__: Write to 2nd VRF
34	- __VWn__: Write to n-th (1~8) VRF
35	
36	![vl2r](fig/vl2r.png)
37	
38	In above figure, you can see two pipelines, the upper one is scalar stages, the bottom one is VPU stages.
39	
40	You can also right-click on Konata App and choose `Lane --> Split Lanes` to combine or split these two pipelines.
41	
42	__CAVEAT__: Current approaches __MAY HAVE DEFECTS__, so the Konata Pipeline View __MAY NOT BE 100% CORRECT__.
43

file.content:
## View the Output Log
To visualize the log generated from simulator, download `Konata` from [GitHub](https://github.com/shioyadan/Konata/tree/master). Follow the installation and usage instructions provided in the repository to analyze the log file.

Here are some tips:

Right click on your mouse and choose "hide flush"
![konata_hide_flush](fig/konata_hide_flush.png)

To see IPC (instruction per cycle) and other useful statistics
![konata_stat](fig/konata_stat.png)

## Scalar Pipeline Analysis Example

For 45 series CPU, `konata.py` draws 5 scalar stages:
- __IS__: Issue
- __EX__: Execute
- __MM__: Memory
- __LX__: Late Execute
- __WB__: Write Back

In below example, the loop jump is mispredicted in the first time (red underline), so several instructions are flushed (dark region) in between.
But in the next iteration, the jump instruction hits (yellow underline), so no flushed insn anymore.

![konata_pipe](fig/konata_pipe.png)

## Vector Pipeline Analysis Example

For 45-series with VPU, you can see VPU stages
- __VQ__: enqueue to VIQ
- __VD__: dispatch to FU
- __VC__: FU commits
- __VW1__: Write to 1st VRF
- __VW2__: Write to 2nd VRF
- __VWn__: Write to n-th (1~8) VRF

![vl2r](fig/vl2r.png)

In above figure, you can see two pipelines, the upper one is scalar stages, the bottom one is VPU stages.

You can also right-click on Konata App and choose `Lane --> Split Lanes` to combine or split these two pipelines.

__CAVEAT__: Current approaches __MAY HAVE DEFECTS__, so the Konata Pipeline View __MAY NOT BE 100% CORRECT__.

input.old_string: __CAVEAT__: Current approaches __MAY HAVE DEFECTS__, so the Konata Pipeline View __MAY NOT BE 100% CORRECT__.
input.new_string:
__CAVEAT__: Current approaches __MAY HAVE DEFECTS__, so the Konata Pipeline View __MAY NOT BE 100% CORRECT__.

## Loop-Window Pipeline Dump

For long-running workloads, a full Kanata/Clarity dump of the entire simulation is unwieldy: the file is large and the interesting steady-state behavior is buried among warmup, OS code, and cache-cold iterations. The loop-window feature restricts the dump to a chosen number of iterations of one specific loop body, after a configurable warmup, then exits the simulator.

### When to use it

- You have identified a hot loop and want to inspect its steady-state pipeline behavior in Konata.
- You want IPC for the loop body alone, ignoring warmup and tail effects.
- You need a small, shareable pipeline trace instead of a multi-GB log.

### Flags

All flags are optional and only take effect when combined with `--kanata` or `--clarity`. `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must be specified together.

| Flag | Argument | Default | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `--kanata-loop-start` | `<pc>` | - | Start PC of the loop body. Reported in the summary; not used to gate iterations. Hex (`0x...`) or decimal accepted. |
| `--kanata-loop-end`   | `<pc>` | - | End PC of the loop body. Each retire of this PC counts as one iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-skip`  | `<N>`  | 0 | Warmup iterations to ignore before the window opens. `0` means record from the first iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-record`| `<X>`  | 1 | Iterations to capture after warmup. Must be >= 1. The simulator exits as soon as this many iterations have retired. |

### How iterations are counted

An iteration is counted only when the instruction at `--kanata-loop-end` __retires__. Mispredicted or flushed fetches of that PC do not advance the counter, so the recorded window is always exactly `record` complete iterations of the loop body.

### Picking the start/end PCs

Use `objdump -d` on your ELF and pick:

- `start` = first instruction of the loop body (typically the branch target / loop label).
- `end`   = last instruction of the loop body (typically the back-branch, or the instruction immediately before it if the back-branch is conditional and you want only taken iterations).

Both PCs must be reachable in the simulated execution. If `end` is never retired, the window never opens and the simulator runs to completion as usual.

### Output

While the gate is closed (warmup), no `I` / `C` / `R` records are emitted to the Kanata/Clarity file, but the cycle counter still advances internally so timing inside the window is accurate. When the window closes, `spdlog` reports:

```
Kanata loop window [pc 0x...start..0x...end]: <iters> iters, <insns> insns, <cycles> cycles, IPC=<ipc>
```

and the simulator exits with status 0. The dump file contains a complete, self-consistent Kanata trace for just those iterations and can be opened directly in Konata.

### Example

```
vsim --elf bench.elf \
     --kanata bench.kanata \
     --kanata-loop-start 0x80001234 \
     --kanata-loop-end   0x80001260 \
     --kanata-loop-skip  100 \
     --kanata-loop-record 8
```

Skips the first 100 iterations of the loop, records the next 8, prints the IPC summary, and exits. `bench.kanata` contains those 8 iterations only.

### Constraints

- `--kanata-loop-*` requires `--kanata` or `--clarity`; otherwise startup fails.
- `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must both be present or both absent.
- `--kanata-loop-record` must be >= 1.
- `--kanata` and `--clarity` remain mutually exclusive.

content.content: The file /local/nick/vsim/docs/PIPELINE.md has been updated successfully.

## Loop-Window Pipeline Dump

For long-running workloads, a full Kanata/Clarity dump of the entire simulation is unwieldy: the file is large and the interesting steady-state behavior is buried among warmup, OS code, and cache-cold iterations. The loop-window feature restricts the dump to a chosen number of iterations of one specific loop body, after a configurable warmup, then exits the simulator.

### When to use it

- You have identified a hot loop and want to inspect its steady-state pipeline behavior in Konata.
- You want IPC for the loop body alone, ignoring warmup and tail effects.
- You need a small, shareable pipeline trace instead of a multi-GB log.

### Flags

All flags are optional and only take effect when combined with `--kanata` or `--clarity`. `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must be specified together.

| Flag | Argument | Default | Meaning |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| `--kanata-loop-start` | `<pc>` | - | Start PC of the loop body. Reported in the summary; not used to gate iterations. Hex (`0x...`) or decimal accepted. |
| `--kanata-loop-end`   | `<pc>` | - | End PC of the loop body. Each retire of this PC counts as one iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-skip`  | `<N>`  | 0 | Warmup iterations to ignore before the window opens. `0` means record from the first iteration. |
| `--kanata-loop-record`| `<X>`  | 1 | Iterations to capture after warmup. Must be >= 1. The simulator exits as soon as this many iterations have retired. |

Use `objdump -d` on your ELF and pick:

- `start` = first instruction of the loop body (typically the branch target / loop label).
- `end`   = last instruction of the loop body (typically the back-branch, or the instruction immediately before it if the back-branch is conditional and you want only taken iterations).

Both PCs must be reachable in the simulated execution. If `end` is never retired, the window never opens and the simulator runs to completion as usual.

```
vsim --elf bench.elf \
```

- `--kanata-loop-*` requires `--kanata` or `--clarity`; otherwise startup fails.
- `--kanata-loop-start` and `--kanata-loop-end` must both be present or both absent.
- `--kanata-loop-record` must be >= 1.
- `--kanata` and `--clarity` remain mutually exclusive.

## View the Output Log
To visualize the log generated from simulator, download `Konata` from [GitHub](https://github.com/shioyadan/Konata/tree/master). Follow the installation and usage instructions provided in the repository to analyze the log file.

Here are some tips:

Right click on your mouse and choose "hide flush"
![konata_hide_flush](fig/konata_hide_flush.png)

To see IPC (instruction per cycle) and other useful statistics
![konata_stat](fig/konata_stat.png)

## Scalar Pipeline Analysis Example

For 45 series CPU, `konata.py` draws 5 scalar stages:
- __IS__: Issue
- __EX__: Execute
- __MM__: Memory
- __LX__: Late Execute
- __WB__: Write Back

In below example, the loop jump is mispredicted in the first time (red underline), so several instructions are flushed (dark region) in between.
But in the next iteration, the jump instruction hits (yellow underline), so no flushed insn anymore.

![konata_pipe](fig/konata_pipe.png)

## Vector Pipeline Analysis Example

For 45-series with VPU, you can see VPU stages
- __VQ__: enqueue to VIQ
- __VD__: dispatch to FU
- __VC__: FU commits
- __VW1__: Write to 1st VRF
- __VW2__: Write to 2nd VRF
- __VWn__: Write to n-th (1~8) VRF

![vl2r](fig/vl2r.png)

In above figure, you can see two pipelines, the upper one is scalar stages, the bottom one is VPU stages.

You can also right-click on Konata App and choose `Lane --> Split Lanes` to combine or split these two pipelines.

__CAVEAT__: Current approaches __MAY HAVE DEFECTS__, so the Konata Pipeline View __MAY NOT BE 100% CORRECT__.

+For long-running workloads, a full Kanata/Clarity dump of the entire simulation is unwieldy: the file is large and the interesting steady-state behavior is buried among warmup, OS code, and cache-cold iterations. The loop-window feature restricts the dump to a chosen number of iterations of one specific loop body, after a configurable warmup, then exits the simulator.
+### When to use it
+- You have identified a hot loop and want to inspect its steady-state pipeline behavior in Konata.
+- You want IPC for the loop body alone, ignoring warmup and tail effects.
+- You need a small, shareable pipeline trace instead of a multi-GB log.
+### Flags

+| Flag | Argument | Default | Meaning |

+| `--kanata-loop-end`   | `<pc>` | - | End PC of the loop body. Each retire of this PC counts as one iteration. |
+| `--kanata-loop-skip`  | `<N>`  | 0 | Warmup iterations to ignore before the window opens. `0` means record from the first iteration. |
+| `--kanata-loop-record`| `<X>`  | 1 | Iterations to capture after warmup. Must be >= 1. The simulator exits as soon as this many iterations have retired. |

+Use `objdump -d` on your ELF and pick:
+- `start` = first instruction of the loop body (typically the branch target / loop label).
+- `end`   = last instruction of the loop body (typically the back-branch, or the instruction immediately before it if the back-branch is conditional and you want only taken iterations).
+Both PCs must be reachable in the simulated execution. If `end` is never retired, the window never opens and the simulator runs to completion as usual.

+and the simulator exits with status 0. The dump file contains a complete, self-consistent Kanata trace for just those iterations and can be opened directly in Konata.

lastPrompt: write a docuement to explain the new Kanata loop-window pipeline dump feature for usage.

content.text: Appended a "Loop-Window Pipeline Dump" section to `docs/PIPELINE.md:42` covering: when to use it, the four flags (`--kanata-loop-start/-end/-skip/-record`), retire-based iteration counting, how to pick PCs from `objdump`, the spdlog summary line + exit behavior, an end-to-end example, and the validation constraints from `src/main.cpp`.
